sentences.text: Were we-- we were, [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] at that point in time, in 1943, the rails went through into Theresien.

And the cattle wagons were standing there, and there was a big board from the-- from the road, if you will, from wherever, into the cattle wagons, because the cattle wagons are-- the openings are high.
And when the wagon was full, so they sealed it.
They locked it, and they-- locked it up.
And then, until the whole train was done, which took about three hours, four hours, I have-- it took a long time.
A whole day, I think.
Then we left.
It was dark.
And the engines, to this day, I can't hear that the-- the locomotive makes a very-- made a very special sound, it's an Auschwitz sound.
I can't reproduce it.
It's sort of a mourning sound that they made.
And in that wagon, there were about 100 people and one pail for bodily needs, and one pail with water, which was gone in no time, because the train shook and the water spilled.
And there was a little window, high up, which was behind bars, a small window, but not-- not open.
But there was a window.
And once the train stopped and somebody climbed over somebody's shoulders and looked out, and we realized that we were going through [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] the eastern part of the protectorate.
It was a city where there were many coal mines.
We were going east, that was obvious.
We knew where we were.
And then-- I don't know how long the journey took.
There were people and there was crying.
And we were holding on to each other.
And people finally relieved themselves where they were because they couldn't help it.
And then the train stopped, whether it took two days, one day, three days, I don't know.
I really wouldn't know, because time stopped.
And the-- they unlocked the wagons.
And there were such strange figures running around the platform.
They were all in blue and white stripes with sort of French berets also on their heads, blue and white stripes and high boots.
And everywhere was snow.
It was cold.
It was winter.
And wherever you looked, in spite of that it was a war and that there was-- had been, you know, the-- no lights, because of planes, you know-- that's what we were told.
It had to be properly-- you shouldn't-- in Theresien, you couldn't even have a tiny, tiny, tiny sign of light.
Everything had to be blacked out.
Here there were miles upon miles of barbed wire with lights on them, and watchtowers.
And dogs were barking everywhere.
But mostly the lights amazed me, that I remember.
And somebody asked the guys who spoke Polish who were running around the platform and screaming in German, alles raus, alles raus, everybody out.
And somebody asked, where are we?
And this Polish guy said, [NON-ENGLISH].
And he made like that.
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] So then, alles raus, alles [GERMAN], everything has to be left.
You just get out.
So they put us into some sort of barracks where there were showers.
And they said, take off your clothes, have a shower, and put on the clothes that were hanging on the ot-- in the other room and leave your clothes.
So you lost all your clothes, your shoes, your everything.
And there were rags that you put on.
And then they said, men separate, women separate, and-- I think that for us they brought trucks.
And they took us to a camp.
And don't forget that, in that camp where they took us, there were people whom we knew very well from September, from Theresien.
You know, we had people who we knew quite well.
And so they enlightened us a little bit.
They were not very kind to us.
They were mean.
And you went into barracks as you-- as you disembarked from the-- from the truck.
So they shoved you, always, into a barrack.
And the barrack was a-- a structure where you had-- there is a picture of it there, broken-- where you had a stove or an oven.
And through the whole barrack there went sort of a brick chimney, about that high.
And from the other side, they also could heat it, if they heated it, you know?
And there were bunks, three tier bunks, and five to a tier.
So-- and one thin blanket each.
That was that.
And two pails up front and two pails in the back.
And in the-- at the entrance, there was a-- [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] a room where the block leader slept.
And on the other side was a room where the-- the registrar of the block and the block alteste-- vice block alteste slept.
So those were very prominent positions.
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS]
I think like that normally you could-- three.
Two, three.
I would say the equivalent of a king size bed, queen-- queen size.
Like the bed that's here, not bigger.
But it was board, you know.
No mattress, no nothing.
It was a board.
No straw or anything.
Just a board and a thin blanket.
And [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] in the-- in Auschwitz, there was mostly yelling.
You know, if you remember Auschwitz, everybody yelled.
Everybody screamed.
Nobody talked.
And [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] in the morning, they brought you, from the kitchen, some sort of warm, colored water, and you got your [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] things for the day, your rations for the day.
You got a slice of bread and a slice of-- small slice of margarine.
And at lunch, or whatever, you-- you got a piece, the-- a little watery soup.
You had-- you had a-- they gave you a-- some sort of a metallic bowl and a spoon.
And if you lost the bowl or lost the spoon, you were finished, because nobody would lend you a bowl, and nobody would lend you a spoon, and nobody-- unless it was your bosom friend.
And the bathroom you went, there-- there were wash-- latrines.
And they were like, you know, [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] it was a block of-- of-- and there were holes next to each other.
That was that.
On both sides.
No, no, no, no, mixed.

And-- [PAUSES FOR 5 SECONDS] and there was a couple and who was a big man, who used to be a-- you know, while they still-- in Czechoslovakia, like everywhere else in the world at that time, there were executions.
And he was the chief executioner.
It's true.
And he-- he would-- and he was a little crooked.
And he would yell, in [INAUDIBLE] always, in the-- in this washroom or this-- latrine room, shit fast, shit fast, the queue is growing.
The queue is growing.
Out, out, out.
So that was that.
People shat where they could.
Clean, it wasn't.
Then the-- then this SS men would come in and would yell that it's not clean.
There was no paper, no nothing.
There was a washroom, a washroom at the end of the camp with a-- with a little water trickling.
That was it.
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS]
And there was this soup.
They brought in this soup and people fought over the soup.
They fought of the-- over the scraping of the barrel.
People fought.
They were turned into animal-like creatures.
And then you had people who were heads of the blocks.
There were girls who were leaders of the blocks, and their vice-leaders and the registrars.
And they-- they had boyfriends who were in the kitchen or who were in the-- in the men's block, or who were in the bakery.
And-- and it was OK.
They-- they have-- they gave them-- or they were kapo in the next camp.
And they brought food and they brought bread and they-- they were-- they-- the shoes were the main problem, because of the mud.
Who had shoes was fine.
And the children, that was-- that was a block with children.
And that was-- that was very sad.
Because in the beginning-- [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] you see, I-- I-- I witnessed it twice.
At first the children from the-- from this September transport were gassed in March.
And then we got-- and the children from our transport were not there because it would have been overcrowded.
So then the children from our transport came.
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] That was after they left.
[PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] Anyhow.
They were all gassed.
They were all gassed, except one who was 14 years old, and her mother smuggled her out.
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] The mothers were told, once the men left, you know, if I'm regressing-- first of all, when we came to Auschwitz, within three days, two days, we knew where we were.
We knew that this it it.
This is a death factory, and that there is no other way to call it, and-- because these gas chambers, there were four of them.
They were literally from here to [INAUDIBLE] Street from us, you know?
They-- they were there, around the corner.
Brzezinka, Birkenau, means a birch wood.
And they were hidden in a small birch wood that they left there.
And how-- you didn't know about the chambers because the chambers were very low buildings.
And you-- you had no chance to come close to them.
What you saw were these chimneys that were-- I don't know, I don't want to exaggerate.
But they were 20 meters high, 50 meters high, whatever.
Like this building.
And when they had an, as they called it, Accion, an action, so these chimneys were-- that's where they burned, you know?
The-- the-- the-- the flames were skyrocketing sky high.
The-- the-- the smoke.
And we were constantly covered with-- with this human ashes, you know, like very fine.
So there was no way you could miss it.
Mind you, you could miss it.
My mother, for instance, wouldn't believe it.
She-- she blocked it to such an extent, she wouldn't believe it, that-- that-- she said, don't talk about it, it's not true.
And-- and there were many like her.
Good for them, you know?
Anyway, when these 1,500 men really left and we saw them boarding the train because we were so-- you know-- when they marched and when they boarded the train and they left, everything was wonderful.
And we-- [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] maybe I should-- digress.
You wanted to hear when we were tattooed, because that's part of my story, too.
We were tattooed about a week later, after we arrived to Auschwitz.
And we were tattooed by Polish girls who came from the women's camp and tattooed us.
And there was-- and the girl-- the girl who tattooed me and my friend, her name was Helena.
And she said, if you-- and we started to talk.
And she said she has been in that camp forever.
And she was human.
And she said, if you ever need anything, I am in the woman's camp.
And-- and look me up.
I'll help you.
[PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] And then we-- and that was that.
So after the-- so I am coming back to-- to our story.

After the men left, at about three, four days later, [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] the mothers of the children were called together by-- [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] I don't know whether it was an SS man, or by somebody from the registry.
I-- I don't know.
But they were told that these children are doomed.
And the mother had a choice to go with them, or to go through the selection, which will take place on Sunday.
This was, I don't know, Thursday, Wednesday.
And it's up to them.
It-- if they go through the selection, there is a chance that they will make it and they'll go-- and they will be working in Germany.
And by that time, he believed it, because the men left.
And if they don't go through the selection, they could go and die with their children.
Nobody made any bones about it.
In plain language.
There were 1,000 children.
And three mothers didn't go with them.
So that's remarkable.
None.
I-- I loved them.
Yeah, but I-- I-- I wasn't their mother.
Yes.
Yes.
I worked with the children.
I taught them how to read and write.
A group of children, you know.

They were-- we divided them by age into small groups, into groups who-- who-- just a play school and some were older children-- I mean, older, eight year old, nine year old, six year old, we tried to teach how to read and write from little things that the men or the people who worked in the-- in the central warehouse where all the things from the people who came from all over the world were stored.
So that was called Kanada.
And we-- they brought us books, some books.
Whatever they could.
You know, that-- it was unlimited there.
They were sorting the clothes and they were being packed to Germany, for people who were bombed out or for-- they called it winter help, the help for the winter.
It was all going back to the Reich, what they took from the Jews.
And the gold that they found, that was going into the Reich's bank, to the central bank in Berlin.

So-- and the gold teeth that they took out because once the people were gassed, so there was a commander that didn't do anything but took out-- opened their mouth and took out the gold teeth, gold-- everything, you know, with pliers.
And that was also sent, then, to Germany.
I don't know where it was melted.
I am sure it wasn't melted in Auschwitz.
People stole.
People had gold, you know, who worked with us.
And for that you could have a girl, buy a girl, buy bread, buy boots, buy gloves, hats, scarves, food.
There was food.
People came with-- with tins and with smoked meat, you know.
That-- there was everything.
So what-- it-- [INAUDIBLE] so officially it was supposed to go to Germany.
Of course, people who worked there stole.
If they were caught, too bad, but mostly they were not caught.
For the gold, yes.
That was-- but then some of the SS men, you know, there was a big business going.
They bribed the inmates to steal gold for them.
And they would share some of it.
So [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] it was a world unto itself.
I don't know.
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] We got up.
We were just as tired as the night before.
And-- because at-- at night there wasn't the added attraction that you couldn't go-- leave the barrack.
You couldn't go anywhere to the bathroom.
So there were two pails for these 500 women, in front and in back.
And I don't know.
You-- you-- you got-- you put on what you had, if you took something off at night.
I don't remember.
And you waited for the warm coffee, warm water, and for the piece of bread and for the margarine.
And then I walked into the children's barrack and I spent the day over there.
It was lucky for me that I didn't have to go anywhere.
And then at night, I visited with my mother.
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] And the-- the women were mostly sitting on the bunks.
You were allowed to go out, walk on-- up and down the Lagerstrasse, as they call it, I don't know, for sometime during the day.
But certainly you were-- you were supposed to look busy, like carry something from here to there so that they don't attack you that you are working.
But there was no work.
There was-- you-- people carried stone from one place to another.
There was nothing to work on, but-- yet you were supposedly working.
[INTERPOSING VOICES]
Well, it-- it was empty.
There were no-- there was no tier.
They were-- they were little-- the-- the men in the-- in the-- there was a work shop.
So they made them little tables and little stools.
And-- and they played with woods.
And then, from this Kommando Kanada, they brought some toys, they-- the way they brought toy-- they brought books, and they brought coloring books, and pencils.
And slowly, but we had stuff.
Not much, but we had a little.
And then when it got nice, we would play-- we would play outside, which was almost at the wire, because it was already March and it was-- this was later.
It was April and it was May.
And it was getting warmer and the mud was getting muddier, because whatever frost there was, it defrosted.
But it was-- it was easier to be outside.
It wasn't so cold.
It wasn't so bitter cold.
The sun sometimes shone-- shining.
So it helped.
And you saw the selections.
I mean, you-- it was like a big theater.
Because we were right behind the railway.
And there was the wire, and there was a piece of no man's land, And then there was the rail and the platform.
So you saw it.
And there were-- there was a-- in May, the transports from Hungary came.
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] Thousands upon thousands of them, every day.
And they burned 20,000 people, 10,000 people each day in May.
All the people from Hungary, and very few got out.
And the ones that got out, they were in the camp next to us, and they shaved them.
They were shaved already.
I don't know why.
They do-- we still weren't shaved.
And [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] so that was a-- that was really killing en masse.
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] And that about 10,000 people a day.
There were about 340,000 Jews in May, from Hungary.
Nothing.
People just-- frightened people coming out.
And then going, because that was-- you know, you just saw the wagons.
And you might have seen some of the SS officers from the back.
We were behind them.
You know?
So I can't say it was that one or it was that one.
But we knew exactly what was going on, absolutely, because these-- these chimneys, they worked 24 hours a day, the flames.
And then they couldn't manage it anymore.
So they-- they-- the burned them outside of the crematoria.
The stench was unbearable.
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] It was awful.
Anyhow, we didn't see many SS in the camp, because they were afraid of the lice and of the sickness and whatever.
There was always one guy who was-- we call [NON-ENGLISH].
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] He was in charge, you know, of the daily life, I guess, of the camp.
And then there was somebody who we-- who was called the [NON-ENGLISH], the sanitary officer.
I don't know what he did there.
But he walked around.
He wasn't bad.
He didn't yell.
This [NON-ENGLISH] yelled.
So we didn't see that many, you know, when there was an inspection, so that was-- everybody had to disappear in the barracks.
And the blockalteste had to salute.
And-- [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] and then they walked through the block.
And if there was a speck of dust on the mud floor, somebody would get it.
So that's about how it worked.
Eva, we're going to take a break.
OK.
